source_metadata.title_raw: Gary B. Bell - A-19-232 - February 27, 2020 - Grass Valley

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Gary B. Bell 
Town Attorney 
Town of Yountville 
420 Sierra College Drive, Suite 140 
Grass Valley, CA 95945-5091 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-232 
Dear: Mr. Bell: 
February 27, 2020 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the Town of Yountville 
regarding Government Code Section 1090, et seq. 1 Please note that we are only providing advice 
under Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law 
conflict of interest, including Public Contract Code. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
We are required to forward your request regarding Section 1090 and all pertinent facts 
relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the County District Attorney's Office, 
which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either 
entity. (Section 1097.1 ( c )( 4 ). ) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 
1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other 
than the requester." (See Section 1097.l(c)(5).) 
QUESTION 
Does the Town's proposed transfer of surplus property to Councilmember Jeffrey Durham 
after Councilmember Durham leaves office violate Section 1090? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-19-232 
Page No, 2 
Yes. So long as Councilmember Durham is a member of the Town Council, Section 1090 
prevents the Town from making any decision to transfer property to Councilmember Durham 
including any decision that will result in the future transfer of the property to the Councilmember. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
Steve Rogers is the Town Manager of Yountville, a position appointed by the Town 
Council. The Town, like most cities, has adopted the "city manager form of government" under 
Government Code Section 34851 et seq. in which the Town Manager manages the Town's 
administrative functions and day-to-day operations.2 As the person charged with day-to-day 
operations and the efficient administration of the Town's affairs, he oversees among all other things 
the sale, purchase, and lease of Town-owned real property. 
In this role, the Town Manager has identified a small, undevelopable piece of surplus right-
of-way that has never been used for its intended transportation purposes - it runs between four 
parcels, was originally intended as a through street as far back as the 1960's, and was never 
developed as such. One of the property owners is Councilmember Durham. The property is 
approximately 35 feet by 317 feet and approximately 12,945 square feet in total, and is 
undevelopable because it contains a seasonal creek running entirely through it and comprising most 
of its square footage. The Town's ownership of the real property raises liability concerns from 
flooding, fire, and other common occurrences of ownership such as trespassing. The real property 
cannot easily be accessed without crossing private property and therefore cannot easily be 
monitored. In short, the Town cannot use this property for any useful purpose and its continued 
ownership of the property raises liability concerns. For these reasons, the Town Manager seeks a 
plan to relinquish ownership of the real property. 
Under Streets and Highways Code Section 8331, this type of right-of-way may be 
"summarily vacated" (i.e., relieved of its original purpose) pursuant to the procedures in Streets and 
Highways Code Section 8335 et seq. Only the Town Council may vacate this right-of-way by 
adopting a resolution of vacation. The Town Manager intends to recommend to the Town Council 
the summary vacation of this right-of-way. Because of the proximity of this real property to 
Councilmember Durham's property, Councilmember Durham will not participate in the decision to 
summarily vacate in any way up to and including the vote. 
The Town does not plan to transfer any property to Councilmember Durham while he is on 
the Town Council or otherwise affiliated with the Town in any respect. Staff has not and will not 
discuss this plan with him while he is in office. The Town Council has delegated to the Town 
Manager the authority to sign, deliver, and record grant deeds without approval of the Town 
Council. Thus, assuming the Town Council -without Councilmember Durham's participation-
summarily vacates the right-of-way, the real property will be surveyed, divided, and transferred to 
the three other private property owners excluding Councilmember Durham without the Town 
Council's involvement in any way. The Town Manager would then transfer the remaining parcel to 
2 The specific duties of the Town Manager are found in Section 34851 et seq. and, more specifically, 
Yountville Municipal Code section 2.08.010 et seq. 


File No. A-19-232 
Page No. 3 
Councilmember Durham once he has left office and is no longer a Councilmember. Under the 
proposed plan, Councilmember Durham would recuse himself under the Political Reform Act and 
implementing regulations due to his ownership of real property within 500 feet. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from 
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their 
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Under Section 1090, "the prohibited 
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig 
(1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void, regardless of 
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.3d 633, 646-649.) 
When members of a public board, commission or similar body have the power to execute 
contracts, each member is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of all contracts by 
his or her agency regardless of whether the member actually participates in the making of the 
contract. (Thomson v. Call, supra at pp. 645 & 649; Fraser-Yamor Agency, Inc. v. County of Del 
Norte (1977) 68 Cal.App.3d 201; 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 49 (2006).) 
Council Participation in the Contract 
When a governmental board ultimately has the authority over the contracts made by a 
subordinate decision maker, generally the board members have participated in the resulting contract 
thereby triggering Section 1090 by exercising their authority to review or not to review the 
contracts. (See City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey, supra, 103 Cal.App.3d at p. 195 [ where the city 
council has authority to approve the city's unilateral action to set the rate charged to a concession 
stand, "it is not [the councilmember's] participation in the voting which constitutes the conflict of 
interest [under Section 1090], but her potential to do so"]; 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 122, 124 (2005) [a 
city council has indirectly participated in the city's decisions regarding advertising rates in the 
city's quarterly brochure because "in effect" the city council approves the advertising rates in 
approving the proposed revenue derived from advertising specified in the city budget]; 87 
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 9 (2004) [the governing board of a school district may not avoid Section 1090 by 
adopting a policy delegating to the district superintendent its authority to contract on behalf of the 
district].) 
Here, the Town Council would first need to summarily vacate the right-of-way for the 
property in question by adopting a resolution of vacation, which only it can do. The Town Council 
has delegated to the Town Manager the authority to sign, deliver, and record grant deeds without 
approval of the Town Council. As such, because the ultimate power to execute the contracts rests in 
the Town Council, Councilmember Durham is considered to have participated in the contract the 
agency makes concerning the transfer of title to the property in question despite the fact that the 
Town Council has delegated the authority to sign and record deeds to the Town Manager. 


Transfer as a Contract 
File No. A-19-232 
Page No. 4 
To determine whether a contract is involved in the decision, one may look to general 
principles of contract law (84 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 34, 36 (2001); 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230,234 
(1995) ), while keeping in mind that "specific rules applicable to Sections 1090 and 1097 require 
that we view the transactions in a broad manner and avoid narrow and technical definitions of 
'contract."' (People v. Honig, supra, at p. 351 citing Stigall, supra, at pp. 569, 571.) "It is essential 
to a contract that there should be: 1. Parties capable of contracting; 2. Their consent; 3. A lawful 
object; and, 4. A sufficient cause or consideration." (Civ. Code, § 1550.) A contract is "made" on 
mutual assent of the involved parties. (Stigall, supra, at p. 569.) No contract is "made" and no duties 
attach until one party has offered and another has accepted and consideration is exchanged. But 
before this, there are "negotiations, discussions, reasoning, planning, and give and take," all of 
which are instrumental in "making" the agreement. (Stigall, supra, at p. 569.) 
According to your facts, the Town's ownership of the real property raises liability concerns 
from flooding, fire, and other common occurrences of ownership such as trespassing, and is 
undevelopable because it contains a seasonal creek running entirely through it and comprising most 
of its square footage. The Town cannot use this property for any useful purpose. For these reasons, 
the Town Manager seeks a plan to relinquish ownership of the real property. In accepting the 
property, the recipient landowner is reliving the Town from its liability concern as well as the need 
to monitor and maintain of the property. As we interpret contracts broadly for purposes of Section 
1090, this is a sufficient exchange of consideration constituting a contract. 
, 
Pursuant to these facts and based on the authority cited above, we find that Councilmember 
Durham's participation in the making of the contract is presumed because the Town Council has 
delegated the authority to transfer surplus Town property to the Town Manager; Section 1090 
therefore prohibits the Town from making this agreement so long as Councilmember Durham is a 
member of the Town Council. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

Does the Town's proposed transfer of surplus property to Councilmember Jeffrey Durham 
after Councilmember Durham leaves office violate Section 1090?

prevents the Town from making any decision to transfer property to Councilmember Durham 
including any decision that will result in the future transfer of the property to the Councilmember.
